topic: Someone finds their parent's draft letter of resignation from decades ago
raw_output:
[story 1]

The brittle, yellowed paper felt alien in my hands. It was tucked inside the lining of an old steamer trunk I’d inherited from a great-aunt I barely remembered. As I unfolded the document, the familiar, looping script of my father swam into focus. A resignation letter. The date was from thirty years ago. My breath hitched, catching somewhere low in my chest. My fingers trembled, making the edges of the paper crinkle audibly. What was I supposed to do with this? The air in the attic suddenly felt too thick, pressing down on my shoulders. I reread the final sentence, the formality of the language mocking the sudden, sharp drop in my stomach. Every muscle in my neck tightened as I tried to process the sudden weight of that long-ago decision.

[story 2]

I kept glancing over my shoulder, half expecting someone to walk in and see me holding this faded piece of stationery. The ink was faded, the paper thin enough to tear with a single, careless pull. Reading the words—my mother’s signature, the formal phrasing of departure—sent a cold tremor through my limbs. My hands started to shake uncontrollably, forcing me to grip the letter so tightly my knuckles turned white. I dropped it onto the dusty floorboards, the sound echoing disproportionately loud in the silence of the room. I stood frozen, staring at the spot where the letter lay, my heart hammering a frantic rhythm against my ribs, wishing with a sudden, desperate intensity that I could just disappear.

[story 3]

The light in the study seemed too harsh, illuminating the precise, faded handwriting on the page. It was my father’s handwriting, a script I thought I knew intimately, yet now it felt like a foreign language spoken by a stranger. I traced a line with my fingertip, the sensation sending a shiver up my arm that had nothing to do with the room temperature. My gaze darted from the signature to the date, then back again, a rapid, repetitive motion. A tightness began to constrict around my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth making the action feel laborious, as if I were trying to force something down that wouldn't settle. The implications of this single piece of paper settled over me like a heavy shroud.
